en: Hello, Sharks. My name is Carrie Schultz-Haslip, and I am here today seeking $100,000 for 10%
equity in my company. Sharks, when my niece was born, I watched my brother and sister-in-law
struggle to get her in and out of baby clothes. Her head was big, her neck was fragile, and
her little arms fought them every step of the way. She would cry. They would cry. Even
I wanted to cry. It was so stressful. All I could think was, why are these ding-dongs
trying to stuff her into a sausage casing six times a day? Why didn't they buy the ones
that avoid her head and arms altogether? But I quickly realized baby clothes like that
didn't exist, so I created them. Meet Tabby's, the easy-peasy, no more squeezy, fumble-free
baby clothes. We're the first ever bottom-up bodysuit designed to dress your baby without
the drama. It's fast, it's foolproof, and it's guaranteed fuss-free. Tabby's goes on
feet first and slides up to snap at the shoulders. Our proprietary shoulder snaps and quick-snap
finger pockets make dressing even the wriggliest of babies a breeze. Whether it's kicking,
squirming, or gator rolls, Tabby's has got your baby covered, or uncovered, because Tabby's
also slides down for all those messy moments. Tabby's believes every parent and child deserves
less struggles and more snuggles. So, Sharks, who wants to help me take this bottom-up idea
to the top? In front of you are some Tabby's to try out for yourselves. Nobody has thought
of this? There is nothing out there like this? No. Is there anything without a snap, but
a Velcro or something else? Nope. So nothing opens up from the top? So everything is traditionally
just open to the bottom, correct? Yes. I'm really intrigued by this zipper. Yeah. I mean,
this is really cool. The only thing I worry about is my babies were all a little chubby.
You know, they had little rolls. Like, would their little rolls get stuck in the zipper?
Oh, no, no. Let me assure you, I'm a former architect. I have a master's degree in architecture.
Amazing. So anything I touch or can design or create happens to within an inch of its
life. So from architecture to children's... So you are an architect. ...to baby clothes.
Correct. Let's get back to this business. So a 10%, $100,000 million valuation, that's
because you have sales. I do. Last year, we did $26,000 net product sales. And this year,
so far, we've done $17,000. Okay, that's not a lot. You are correct. It is not a lot, which
is exactly why I'm here, because while I may be very good at designing things and solving
I can see that. Do you have a patent on this? I do. A utility? I have pending utility patents
on the tee, the romper, and the bodysuit. And I have three granted design patents. So
you've done $26,000 in sales. How are you getting these sales today? What have you done
for marketing to even get that? It's mostly word of mouth and social media. Well, let
me tell you why I even came to this, really. Yes. So my brother and sister-in-law had their
daughter and I went to help them out. And I was like a whiz with babies. I've been babysitting
since I was 12. Well, I'd never had to do it 10 or 12 times a day. So late one night,
I was like, I'm going to be the hero aunt, and I'm going to go online, and I'm going
to buy the ones that snap at the shoulders. And I could not find anything for months.
And I kind of just got obsessed with it, because at the time, my husband and I were going through
the trials and tribulations of trying to have our own family. And for medical reasons, it
was advised that I not carry a child on my own. So we had to go down. How cliche is this?
All the crying. Took us a long time, and we had to go down an alternate route. My daughter
was eventually born from surrogacy. And I'm so sorry. It is incredible to be here. And
so this is like overwhelming good crying. But I became so passionate about it, because
I'd watched her be a new mom and go through that. And I was like, I want something that's
like easy peasy. What would I do that makes it simple? And I just sort of got obsessed
with it. And so you figured it out. I figured it out. To me, it seems like a total licensing
play. We've got an expert here. Daymond John, what do you say? You own 100% of the company?
I own 65%, and I have one other investor, which is my father. How much of your own money
was in? I've put in almost $1 million. Ouch. How much did your dad put in? 35%. And what
did that money go to? I know some to pass. You have to realize this has actually been
going on for seven years, but we've only been in the market a little under two. And we've
only had beyond six months for a year. You've been working on this for seven years? Correct.
Seven years. Anywhere along the line, did you wake up in the morning and say, what the
**** am I doing? No. And how much do you sell these for? The romper's $28. I make it for
$475. The bodysuit is $20. It cost me $2.90. Wow. Okay, Carrie, the binary issue for this
deal, is this innovation something the market would buy if it was properly presented to
it? I don't know the answer to that question. And so that's the problem. I hope you can
convince- Well, before, because I feel, I've seen this movie before. You're about to see
it again. You're going to see it again. I'm out. Oh, that's fair. Okay. I'm shocked that
you burned through a million bucks on this. That's fair. Where did a million go? Last
year, I was like, okay, we got to have someone handle the marketing. We're really doing this.
And I got in this vicious cycle of pitch and ditch. I basically hired a bunch of consultants,
marketing consultants. They were expensive. And they're expensive, and what I-
They all told you how great a job they were going to do, and you were all excited. All right,
I'm out again. Okay, great, great. I can't wait for you to be out a third time after I tell you
this part. So, I'm excited. So, one thing that was very serendipitous is I thought I was solving
this easy dressing problem, right? And then I started getting all of these messages from
parents who had babies and children in the NICU. One in 10 babies will stay in the NICU. When
they're in the NICU, they're almost exclusively naked and wrapped in a blanket, because this is
being monitored. This is being monitored. This is how they're being fed. This is how they're,
like, all of it. And so, to clothe them, you'd have to disconnect all of those things. But because
you can unsnap and snap the shoulders, you can slide it on, you can slide it off,
you don't have to disconnect any equipment. And I'm now in the testing phase, and they want to
order 80,000 units a year to put exclusively in their circuit of NICUs. Well, you probably
should have said something about that earlier. That's pretty impressive. 80,000 units is a lot.
Carrie, that makes me feel better for you. It's a niche market. For me, I think the whole play,
if this is a better mousetrap, no one's done it. You have patents, you have something to sell.
You could go to one of the big baby clothing manufacturers and maybe license them. But it's
not really the right deal for me, so I'm sorry. I'm out. No, no problem. Thank you. Yeah, Carrie,
I'm kind of in the same boat. I mean, I feel bad, but now I feel better. Yeah. You know,
I just don't see how I can add a whole lot of value to this. So, for those reasons, I'm out.
Congrats and good luck. Thank you.
This is a hard one. Your passion is so strong. So, the challenge here is that the $100,000 you're
asking for, forget the 10%, that's obviously not going to happen. I think the best thing
that I can do is probably do a deal with you where we go out and license it to people.
I would take 45%. Whoa. Okay, 45% for $100,000? Correct.
Okay, listen. I mean, I see the licensing. I think, Damon, what he's offering is actually
really great because I do think you're an inventor, right? You invented this really
cool thing, but then in the rest of it, the production, trying to figure out the fulfillment,
I can tell just this huge weight. This is something that, for me, I would buy it for
people that are having a baby, but I don't think I would be able to really help you with
the licensing aspect. So, I'm out. No problem. Thank you so much.
Carrie, you have an offer from Damon. What do you want to do?
You do. I so appreciate that offer, and I wonder if you'd be kind enough to give me
a moment to step away and call my business partner as a courtesy.
Dad. Oh, my dad. Sorry, my dad. Call dad. Yeah, I'm not going to tell you don't call dad. Okay, don't call dad.
Dad's got a lot of money at risk. Okay, thank you so much.
Thank you. Dad?
Before you start, I just want you to know how very, very proud mom and I are of what you've
done. You've just done incredible. No matter what happens, we're very proud of you.
Thanks. I'm proud of you, too. I forgot dad owns a portion of it.
She's at 65%. Maybe crammed down to nothing.
So, I have one offer on the table. It's from Damon. He's offering $100,000.
For 45% of the company, and wants to really go after the licensing side of it.
How do you feel about that?
Okay, talk to dad. You know, 45% is a lot more equity than I'm 100% comfortable giving up,
but if that's what I have to do to do a deal with someone like Damon John,
I respect that and I promise to make you proud. What were you going to go tell her that I said?
I was going to go into the hallway to tell you that Damon realized that your father owns a
decent percentage of the company. So, he's looked at me and he said,
I think I should really have made it 33 and a third percent.
There you go. And then it would be 333.
So, therefore. But I'm going to play tough. Can I get 35? What about 33 and a third?
All right.
